
---
title: "Bayes Rules! – Chapter 2 Solutions"
output: html_document
---

# 📘 Chapter 2: Solutions

## ✏️ Exercise 2.2

Define the following events for a resident of a fictional town:  
A = drives 10 miles per hour above the speed limit,  
B = gets a speeding ticket,  
C = took statistics at the local college,  
D = has used R,  
E = likes the music of Prince, and  
F = is a Minnesotan.

Several facts about these events are listed below. Specify each of these facts using probability notation, paying special attention to whether it’s a marginal, conditional, or joint probability.

(a) 73% of people that drive 10 miles per hour above the speed limit get a speeding ticket.  
(b) 20% of residents drive 10 miles per hour above the speed limit.  
(c) 15% of residents have used R.  
(d) 91% of statistics students at the local college have used R.  
(e) 38% of residents are Minnesotans that like the music of Prince.  
(f) 95% of the Minnesotan residents like the music of Prince.

**Solutions:**

(a) \( P(B \mid A) = 0.73 \)
(b) \( P(A) = 0.20 \)
(c) \( P(D) = 0.15 \)
(d) \( P(D \mid C) = 0.91 \)
(e) \( P(F \cap E) = 0.38 \)
(f) \( P(E \mid F) = 0.95 \)

---

## ✏️ Exercise 2.5

A local arboretum contains a variety of tree species, including elms, maples, and others. Unfortunately, 18% of all trees in the arboretum are infected with mold. Among the infected trees, 15% are elms, 80% are maples, and 5% are other species. Among the uninfected trees, 20% are elms, 10% are maples, and 70% are other species. In monitoring the spread of mold, an arboretum employee randomly selects a tree to test.

**Questions:**

(a) What’s the prior probability that the selected tree has mold?
(b) The tree happens to be a maple. What’s the probability that the employee would have selected a maple?
(c) What’s the posterior probability that the selected maple tree has mold?
(d) Compare the prior and posterior probability of the tree having mold. How did your understanding change in light of the fact that the tree is a maple?

**Solutions:**


(a) \( P(\text{mold}) = 0.18 \)

(b)  
\[
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{maple}) &= P(\text{maple} \mid \text{mold}) \cdot P(\text{mold}) + P(\text{maple} \mid \text{no mold}) \cdot P(\text{no mold}) \\
&= 0.80 \cdot 0.18 + 0.10 \cdot 0.82 = 0.226
\end{aligned}
\]

(c)  
\[
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{mold} \mid \text{maple}) &= \frac{0.80 \cdot 0.18}{0.226} \approx 0.637
\end{aligned}
\]

(d)  
The posterior probability (0.637) is noticeably higher than the prior (0.18), showing how the observation (tree is a maple) increased our belief that the tree has mold.

---

## ✏️ Exercise 2.17

Repeat Exercise 2.5 utilizing simulation to approximate the posterior probability that a randomly selected maple tree has mold. Specifically, simulate data for 10,000 trees and remember to set your random number seed.

```{r}
# Load necessary libraries
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(123)

# Simulate mold status
mold_status <- sample(c("mold", "no_mold"), size = 10000, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.18, 0.82))

# Assign species based on mold status
tree_species <- ifelse(mold_status == "mold",
                       sample(c("maple", "not_maple"), 10000, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.80, 0.20)),
                       sample(c("maple", "not_maple"), 10000, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.10, 0.90)))

# Create data frame
tree_data <- data.frame(mold_status, tree_species)

# Estimate posterior
posterior <- mean(tree_data$mold_status[tree_data$tree_species == "maple"] == "mold")
posterior
```

**Interpretation:**  
The estimated posterior probability using simulation confirms our earlier analytical result: close to 62.5% of maple trees are moldy, given prior information and conditional distributions. Why is the number not exactly 63.7?
